# Enabling Gmail & Calendar (Google OAuth)

These two MCP servers let Jarvis read your email and calendar (and, with your
phone's approval, send mail or create events). They need **your** Google
credentials — there's no way around this, because the access is to *your*
account. The code is wired and waiting; this is the ~10-minute Google side.

## Why you do this part, not me
Reading your Gmail requires an OAuth credential tied to your Google account,
created in your Google Cloud Console. A credential is a secret that proves
"this app may act for *this* person." It must be generated by you and stays
on your machine. Anyone who tells you they can pre-make it for you is either
wrong or handing you theirs.

## Steps (do once)

1. Go to **console.cloud.google.com** → sign in → create a new project
   (top bar → "New Project" → name it e.g. "jarvis").

2. Enable the APIs: search the top bar for **"Gmail API"** → Enable.
   Repeat for **"Google Calendar API"** → Enable.

3. Configure the consent screen: left menu → **APIs & Services → OAuth
   consent screen** → choose **External** → fill the required name/email →
   on "Scopes" you can skip adding any here → on "Test users" **add your own
   Gmail address** (important: only test users can use an unverified app, and
   that's fine for personal use — no Google review needed).

4. Create the credential: **APIs & Services → Credentials → Create
   Credentials → OAuth client ID** → application type **Desktop app** →
   Create. Click **Download JSON**.

5. Put that file on your machine: rename it to **`gcp-oauth.keys.json`** and
   drop it into the `google-creds/` folder in your jarvis-server project.

## Turn it on

1. In `mcp_config.json`, set `"enabled": true` for `gmail` and/or `gcalendar`.
2. `docker compose up -d --build`
3. First run does a one-time browser auth: watch the container logs
   (`docker compose logs -f api`). The Gmail server will print a URL — open
   it, approve access with your Google account, and it saves a
   `credentials.json` back into `google-creds/`. After that it's silent.

## Use it (from your phone)
- "Jarvis, any new emails from my professor?" → reads (auto).
- "Jarvis, what's on my calendar tomorrow?" → reads (auto).
- "Jarvis, email Ravi that I'll be late" → **pops Allow/Deny on your phone**
  before anything is sent. Same for creating/deleting calendar events.

## Safety
Reading is `auto` (silent); sending mail, deleting mail, and creating/
deleting events are `confirm` — they always ask your phone first. The OAuth
files live in `google-creds/`, which is gitignored, so they never reach
GitHub. Scope the consent to only Gmail + Calendar; don't grant more.

## If the npx server name has changed
MCP servers move fast. If `@gongrzhe/server-gmail-autoauth-mcp` or
`@cocal/google-calendar-mcp` fail to install, search npm for a current
"gmail mcp server" / "google calendar mcp server", update the `command`/
`args` in `mcp_config.json`, and keep the same policy structure.
